name: Dual Reader Standard — Implementation Guide
headline: How to Implement the Dual Reader Standard for Machine-Verifiable Claims
description: Complete implementation guide for the Dual Reader Standard (DRS), a verification architecture that makes every claim in a knowledge system readable by both human and machine using deterministic falsification predicates encoded in JSON.

about.name: Dual Reader Standard
about.description: A verification architecture for knowledge systems requiring every claim to be readable by two independent reader classes — human and machine.

isPartOf.name: Fracttalix — Dual Reader Standard Implementation Guides

what_is_the_dual_reader_standard.title: What Is the Dual Reader Standard?
what_is_the_dual_reader_standard.summary: The Dual Reader Standard (DRS) is a verification architecture for knowledge systems. Every claim — whether written in prose or implemented in code — must be readable by two independent reader classes: human and machine.

DRP.name: Dual Reader Protocol
DRP.function: Makes prose claims machine-evaluable via 5-part falsification predicates

readers.reader_1: Human — reads the paper in natural language
readers.reader_2: AI — reads the AI layer (structured JSON claim registry)

GVP.name: Grounded Verification Protocol
GVP.function: Makes machine-evaluable claims machine-verified via test bindings and commit-pinned evidence

readers.reader_3a: Coder — reads tier, test_bindings, verified_against fields
readers.reader_3b: Machine — runs pytest, stamps SHA on success

falsification_kernel.title: The Falsification Kernel K = (P, O, M, B)
falsification_kernel.description: The shared foundation of both protocols. Every Type F (falsifiable) claim carries a deterministic predicate that evaluates to exactly one of two verdicts: FALSIFIED or NOT FALSIFIED.

P.name: Predicate
P.role: Logical sentence that, if TRUE, falsifies the claim
constraints: Must evaluate to exactly TRUE or FALSE for any valid variable assignment
Quantifiers range over finite sets only

O.name: Operands
O.role: Typed definitions of every variable in the predicate
constraints: Every free variable in P must appear in O
Every variable in O must appear in P
Each variable must be computable from cited data or derivations

B.name: Bounds
constraints: BOUNDARY specifies inclusive/exclusive for every threshold
CONTEXT justifies every numeric literal in the predicate

example.claim: sort() returns elements in ascending order

predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS i IN range(len(result)-1) SUCH THAT result[i] > result[i+1]
predicate.EVALUATION: Run sort on test vectors; check adjacent pairs; finite
predicate.CONTEXT: Ascending order is the documented contract of sort()

A.name: Axiom
A.description: Foundational premises accepted from literature or stated as starting assumptions. Unfalsifiable by design.

D.name: Definition
D.description: Stipulative definitions of terms, structures, or procedures. Not truth-apt — they define, not claim.

F.name: Falsifiable
F.description: Testable claims with deterministic predicates. The core scientific and software claims.

six_verification_tiers.title: The Six Verification Tiers
six_verification_tiers.description: Every claim carries a tier field declaring what kind of evidence grounds it.

grounded_now.meaning: Exercised by passing tests. test_bindings non-empty, verified_against SHA non-null.
Verified by formal derivation trace or analytical argument

explicitly_ungrounded.meaning: Active placeholder or pending external data. Gap is visible by design.

ai_layer_structure.title: The AI Layer — The Central Artifact
ai_layer_structure.description: A JSON document that accompanies every paper or software system. Contains the full claim registry with predicates, tiers, and test bindings.

required_sections._meta: Document type, schema version, session, licence
required_sections.phase_ready: Phase gate verdict and condition status (c1–c6)
required_sections.claim_registry: Array of all claims with types, predicates, tiers, bindings

phase_gate.title: The Six-Condition Phase Gate
phase_gate.description: A paper or software release is PHASE-READY when all six conditions are satisfied.

conditions.c2: All falsifiable claims registered with predicates
conditions.c5: Verification is self-sufficient (AI layer alone, no prose needed)
conditions.c6: All predicates are non-vacuous (sample falsification observation exists)

how_to_implement.title: How to Implement the Dual Reader Standard

for_a_paper.title: For a Paper (DRP)
steps: Write the paper (prose channel for human readers)
Create the AI layer JSON file (machine channel for AI readers)
Classify every claim as A (axiom), D (definition), or F (falsifiable)
Write the 5-part falsification predicate for every Type F claim
Include a sample_falsification_observation for each Type F claim (vacuity witness)
Assign the verification tier to each claim

for_software.title: For Software (GVP)
steps: Enumerate what the software claims to do
Classify each claim as A (assumption), D (definition), or F (behavioral)
Write the falsification predicate for every Type F claim
Write or identify the tests that exercise each claim
Populate test_bindings with fully qualified test node IDs
Run the tests and record the passing commit SHA in verified_against
Assign the tier: software_tested if tests exist, empirical_pending if not yet
Register any untested claims as placeholders

minimum_viable_adoption.title: Minimum Viable Adoption
minimum_viable_adoption.description: The smallest useful DRS adoption is a single Type F claim with a single test binding.

example.statement: sort() returns elements in ascending order

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS i IN range(len(result)-1) SUCH THAT result[i] > result[i+1]
falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Run sort on test vectors; check adjacent pairs; finite
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Ascending order is the documented contract of sort()

principles.name: Popperian epistemology
principles.description: We can falsify but not verify. A claim that survives all falsification attempts is not proven — it has survived.

principles.name: Honest gaps
principles.description: The placeholder is the most important feature. When a claim is empirical_pending, the system says: we claim this but have not yet verified it. This is strictly more informative than hiding unverified claims.

principles.name: Substrate independence
principles.description: The kernel K = (P, O, M, B) does not know whether it evaluates a scientific theorem or a software guarantee. Future domains (legal, regulatory, policy) can add protocols without modifying the kernel.

principles.name: Machine lingua franca
principles.description: The kernel is written in logic and mathematics, not any human language. A predicate that evaluates to TRUE in Beijing evaluates to TRUE in Boston. JSON is the transport layer. Binary logic is the substrate.

axes.backwards: Nothing already done breaks. Schema versioning, append-only enums, permanent kernel.
axes.lateral: Works across all domains, languages, tools, and AI systems. Substrate-independent kernel.
axes.forward: Anything new can be added without redesign. Protocol extensibility, tier extensibility, additive schema evolution.
